Title: Vestigo confirms fire at offshore Sarawak facility, says no injuries reported
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Vestigo Petroleum Sdn Bhd, a subsidiary of Petronas Carigali, has confirmed that a fire broke out at its West Lutong Vent A (WLV-A) offshore facility off Sarawak yesterday, adding that the situation has been brought under control and no injuries were reported.
The company said the fire occurred at approximately 2pm and that investigations into the cause are ongoing.
The confirmation follows reports of an explosion at the offshore oil and gas platform after a lightning strike hit a vent stack at the facility. A video of the incident later went viral on social media.
In a statement today, Vestigo said it was working closely with the relevant authorities and had taken the necessary precautionary measures to manage any potential risks.
"There were no injuries or personnel affected, and the incident posed no immediate threat to the surrounding communities and environment.
"Vestigo remains fully committed to the safety of its people, protecting the environment, and maintaining the integrity of its operations," it said.
Yesterday, the Fire and Rescue Department said a
lightning
strike had hit a vent stack at the offshore facility, triggering a brief gas line explosion.
Emergency measures, including the closure of a shut-off valve, were immediately carried out to control the situation and prevent further risks. Authorities initially said there was no fire and that all safety procedures had been followed.
Miri district police chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Farhan Lee Abdullah had also said police had yet to receive an official report on the incident but were seeking further information from the contractor operating the platform.
Vestigo said investigations into the cause of the fire were continuing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Homes without window grilles in high-rises put families at risk, says expert
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The growing trend of minimalist, modern homes without window or balcony grilles in high-rise residential buildings is putting occupants and their families at unnecessary risk, an expert has warned.
Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia Department of Architecture and Built Environment lecturer Associate Professor Dr Norngainy Mohd Tawil said many homeowners now prioritise aesthetics, natural lighting and ventilation, opting not to install window grilles or other protective features.
"Minimalist home designs offer many architectural and comfort advantages, but every design decision should be balanced against risk, especially if the home is occupied by children, senior citizens or individuals who require close supervision.
"The beauty of a design should never come at the expense of safety. Homes without grilles can still be made safe by installing features such as window restrictors, laminated safety glass, balcony railings that meet safety specifications, and modern grilles equipped with emergency-release mechanisms," she told Berita Harian.
Norngainy said advances in technology had introduced various safety options, including door and window alarm systems and smart home sensors, but these should not be viewed as substitutes for physical safety measures capable of preventing accidents.
"Alarm systems only provide a warning after an incident has occurred, whereas features such as window restrictors and protective barriers help prevent accidents from happening in the first place.
"In safety design, a well-designed home should not only be visually appealing but also capable of protecting lives," she said.
Based on her observations, children aged below 10 are the group most at risk of accidentally falling from high-rise buildings, while cases involving deliberate jumps are more commonly associated with teenagers and adults.
She said observations of incidents in Malaysia and abroad had revealed several recurring patterns, although each case had its own circumstances and should not be generalised.
"Young children are naturally curious and often climb onto chairs, sofas or other furniture placed near windows and balconies without realising the dangers involved, which can lead to tragedy.
"Many schools today are built with more than five storeys, increasing the need for more comprehensive safety features.
"Children who are playing or being carried while sitting on balcony railings are also exposed to danger if adequate preventive measures are not in place," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Philippines leads the world in rush to solar as power prices soar
Article:
MANILA: People in the Philippines are flocking to install solar power on rooftops and escape the burden of soaring electricity prices, making it the world's biggest spender on solar panels since the war in Iran started.
Top power distributor Meralco has raised prices by 10 per cent since the Middle East conflict began in late February.
Now, a median household spends around 12 per cent of monthly income on electricity, assuming it consumes 200 kilowatt-hours — approximately the monthly average for three people.
The Philippines is one of the few countries in Southeast Asia with barely any power subsidies, and its residential power prices are the highest in the region.
Only Singapore comes close, but its citizens' average purchasing power is nearly 13 times higher.
Adrian Sabatera, a 39-year-old software engineer, thought about getting solar for years but found it too costly.
That changed as costs came down and electricity prices kept rising.
"I wouldn't be shocked if a third of the middle-class population eventually finds their way to this setup," Sabatera said after recently pulling the trigger on a 570,000 peso ($9,300) installation at the Manila house he shares with three others.
The rooftop solar rush has resulted in $407 million in panel imports in the three months through May, a 145 per cent increase from a year earlier, according to trade data from China, which accounts for most global supply.
Even when Chinese panel shipments fell 13 per cent in May after a tax rebate removal, exports to the Philippines rose by almost a third.
On paper, the Netherlands remains a larger market for panels, but experts say that's because it is a transshipment hub.
Surge in inquiries
Philergy German Solar, a Manila-based installer, received more than 2-1/2 times the number of customer enquiries in the first five months of this year compared to last year.
At one point it fielded 3,000 inquiries a day, according to managing partner Jochen Staudter.
Customers are deciding to buy "much faster than before," Staudter said. "Demand will continue to be driven by high electricity prices."
In two years, distributed solar capacity could nearly triple to 3,500 megawatts (MW), matching the current size of the Philippines' utility-scale solar fleet, as loan payback times shrink to 3.1 years from 4 years, said Alnie Demoral, analyst at energy think tank Ember.
Solar accounts for under 4 per cent of national power consumption, government data shows.
Supply challenges
A weakening currency has compounded the increase in power prices because the Philippines relies on imported coal and gas to generate power.
That has pushed inflation to multi-year highs and slowed growth.
Manila entrepreneur Jason Porciuncula installed a 12-kilowatt system with battery storage in January.
As prices hit record highs in May, his monthly bill dropped to a fifth of last summer's 21,000 pesos (RM1,400).
But it's not all smooth sailing. Installations are lagging behind demand due to component hoarding, volatile equipment costs and inadequate quality checks, said Brenda Valerio, Philippines director at New Energy Nexus.
The government provides loans for solar of up to 500,000 pesos (RM33,300) at 5 per cent interest, below market rates. But it excludes private-sector workers.
Another deterrent: high upfront costs, usually above average annual household incomes of 353,200 pesos (RM24,000).
"The opportunity is real, but the upfront cost is often too high for a household or business, no matter how quick the payback time is," Ember's Demoral said. — REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: SMK Derma students impress netizens with professional football livestream skills [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The broadcasting skills of students from SMK Derma in Perlis have taken social media by storm, earning widespread praise from netizens.
The clip, posted on TikTok by the school on June 18, shows students operating a professional livestream for a Perlis inter-school football match.
About 90 per cent of the production was run by the students.
In the video, which has garnered over half a million views, the students were shown handling multiple broadcasting cameras, managing the audio and commentary, and coordinating multi-camera views alongside most of the behind-the-scenes work.
Reddit user @Aoibhealfae lauded the initiative, commenting that it was wonderful for the school to have such advanced equipment to help train students in valuable, real-world skills.
Another netizen, @zet19, agreed, saying it offered invaluable practical experience for any student looking to pursue a career in broadcasting.
"Really cool stuff. Practical experience for the students too if they want to have a go at broadcasting," the user said.

Title: Imported EV prices set to top RM300,000 as new rules take effect
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Fully imported electric vehicles (EVs) are expected to become more expensive in Malaysia from July 1, with showroom prices likely to start from RM300,000 following the implementation of new import requirements by the Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry (Miti).
The policy is expected to reshape the country's EV market by removing affordable imported models from the mass market and encouraging global manufacturers to establish local assembly operations.
Kenanga Research analyst Wan Mustaqim Wan Ab Aziz said the ministry imposed a minimum Cost, Insurance and Freight (CIF) value of RM200,000 for each imported EV unit entering Malaysia.
The new regulations also require a minimum power output requirement of 180 kilowatts (kW) for imported EVs entering the Malaysian market.
He said the new requirements would significantly increase retail prices after accounting for import duties, excise duties, a 10 per cent sales and service tax, and distributor margins.
"This policy effectively ends the market for affordable imported EVs aimed at the mass market, including entry-level models from imported brands such as BYD, GWM (Great Wall Motor) and MG (Morris Garages)," he said in a research note.
However, consumers may still have a limited opportunity to purchase imported EVs at current prices, as CBU vehicles already in the country or in transit before the policy takes effect are exempt from the new requirements until existing inventories are fully sold.
Wan Mustaqim said the import restrictions reflect the ministry's strategy of encouraging global automakers to invest in completely knocked down assembly operations in Malaysia.
"Miti is using these import restrictions to compel global carmakers to invest in CKD production lines, effectively ending reliance on low-cost imports to test the market," he said.
Kenanga Research said the policy would give a competitive advantage to local manufacturers and automakers that have already established local assembly operations.
Among the expected beneficiaries are Perusahaan Otomobil Kedua Sdn Bhd (Perodua) through its Smart Mobility manufacturing facility, as well as Proton and Zeekr models assembled at DRB-Hicom Bhd's Tanjung Malim plant.
Other companies that could benefit include Xpeng, GWM, BAIC (Beijing Automotive Industry Holding), and SAIC (Shanghai Automotive Industry Corp), which utilise EP Manufacturing Bhd's assembly facilities in Melaka, as well as third-party contract manufacturers such as Inokom Corp Sdn Bhd, under Sime Darby Bhd.
Separately, Kenanga Research expects consumer demand to shift towards plug-in hybrid electric vehicles following the expiry of full tax exemptions for imported CBU EVs at the end of last year.
Kenanga Research also cautioned that standard EV assembly lines typically require 30 per cent to 40 per cent fewer labour hours than vehicles powered by internal combustion engines, posing workforce challenges for the automotive industry.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mother longs to look into the eyes of late son's cornea recipient
Article:
PASIR MAS: A mother is hoping to meet the recipient of her late stepson's donated corneas, saying looking into the person's eyes would be the closest she could come to seeing him again.
Nor Afini Din, 42, said she wished to meet the recipient if they were willing to come forward after reading her story.
Her stepson, Muhammad Jaris Amirul Sharulanuar, died on May 23 after the motorcycle he was riding crashed into a road divider near the Kota Iskandar exit in Johor Baru while he was on his way home from work.
He was 28 and would have celebrated his 29th birthday six days later.
Nor Afini said the family was devastated by the loss, but found comfort in fulfilling Muhammad Jaris Amirul's wish to donate his corneas after his death.
She admitted that she and her husband, Sharulanuar Baharom, 56, initially struggled with the decision, but ultimately agreed, believing the donation would become a lasting act of charity (amal jariah) for their son.
"Our entire family, including him, had pledged to become organ donors. I had forgotten about it until my sister, who is a nurse, reminded me before the post-mortem.
"After discussing it with my husband, we agreed to donate both of his corneas before bringing his body home to Kelantan for burial.
"We understand that the recipient's identity must remain confidential, and we accepted that from the beginning. But I continue to pray that one day I will be able to meet the person because I miss him so much. For now, all I can do is visit his grave," she said.
Nor Afini said she had raised Muhammad Jaris Amirul since he was 11 after marrying his father following the death of his biological mother.
She described him as an obedient, kind-hearted and generous eldest brother to his five siblings.
"My husband was often away for work, so I spent most of the time caring for him and his younger siblings. We became very close.
"Even when he wanted to get to know someone, he would ask for my opinion first. If I didn't approve, he would respect my decision.
"He was also a loving elder brother who always tried to fulfil his siblings' wishes whenever he returned home. He was working as an occupational safety and health officer at a company in Johor Baru, so he did not live with us," she said.
Looking back, Nor Afini believes her stepson had shown subtle signs that his time was near, although she only realised their significance after his death.
She said he had been determined to perform the korban sacrifice this year and insisted on giving his contribution well in advance, even though he was due to return home before Hari Raya Aidiladha.
"I told him he could give me the money when he came home, but he insisted on entrusting it to me earlier. Only after he passed away did I realise it was perhaps a sign that he would not make it home.
"His housemate also told us that he was diligent in reciting the Quran. About a month before his death, he started leaving his bedroom door open while reciting. A week before he passed away, he was reciting aloud," she said.
Although still grieving, Nor Afini said she had accepted God's will, believing that God loved her son more.
She said the loss remained deeply painful, especially for the family's youngest child, Muhammad Qarizh Aryan Sharulanuar, 10, who still cries every day and visits his brother's grave twice daily because he misses him dearly.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 13 injured, no deaths in 5.5-magnitude quake in southwest China's Sichuan
Article:
CHENGDU (China): No deaths were reported and 13 people sustained minor injuries as of 3:30Sam Monday after a 5.5-magnitude earthquake struck Gaoxian County in Yibin City, southwest China's Sichuan Province, local authorities said Monday, reported Xinhua.
The injured have been sent to hospitals for treatment, and 196 others have been relocated. Relief efforts are underway in an orderly manner, according to Yibin earthquake relief command headquarters.
The earthquake occurred at 12:12am Monday (Beijing Time).
The epicenter was monitored at 28.50 degrees north latitude and 104.69 degrees east longitude, with a depth of 6km, according to the China Earthquake Networks Centre.
The China Earthquake Administration has initiated a Level-III emergency response following the quake. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit slightly higher against greenback, supported by low oil prices
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit was slightly higher against the US dollar on Monday as the domestic unit continues to be well-supported with lower crude oil prices.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said West Texas Intermediate and Brent crude prices were hovering around US$69.87 per barrel and US$72.46 per barrel, while the United States and Iran are expected to continue their negotiation deals.
At 8am, the local currency was pegged at 4.0855/0950 against the greenback from last Friday's close of 4.0860/0910.
"Shipping continues in the Straits of Hormuz, which suggests that oil shortages could be resolved soon.
"On that note, expect the US dollar/ringgit (pair) to revolve around RM4.08 to RM4.10 today," Mohd Afzanizam told Bernama.
At the opening, the ringgit traded higher against a basket of major currencies.
It advanced against the British pound to 5.3929/4054 from 5.4013/4079 at Friday's close, rose versus the euro to 4.6526/6634 from 4.6605/6662 and was marginally higher vis-à-vis the Japanese yen at 2.5258/5318 from 2.5283/5316 previously.
The local note was traded mixed against regional currencies.
It appreciated against the Singapore dollar to 3.1560/1639 from 3.1581/1625 on Friday's close, was almost flat versus the Indonesian rupiah at 227.9/228.6 from 227.9/228.3, and was flat against the Philippine peso at 6.66/6.68 from 6.66/6.67.
The local note increased versus the Thai baht to 12.2284/2627 from 12.2445/2643 previously.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Father and son rescued after four days buried under rubble of Venezuela's earthquakes
Article:
LA GUAIRA, (Venezuela): A father and his son were pulled out alive from the rubble of a collapsed building on Sunday, four days after the devastating earthquakes that struck Venezuela.
It was a scene that gavehope to the French and US rescue workers active in the area as they race against the clock to find more survivors.
Rescue workers carried the pair, visibly weakened and both wearing masks, on improvised fabric stretchers through debris-strewn streets to a waiting ambulance, as a crowd gathered around the emergency vehicles in La Guaira.
The coastal state was hardest hit by the earthquakes on Wednesday that left at least 1,450 dead and thousands missing.
Their rescue came after 12 hours of painstaking efforts by teams that combed through the ruins using specialized search cameras, carefully working through unstable rubble to reach the trapped victims.
"They are extremely weak, as any patient trapped under rubble for four days would be, so we are doing everything possible to rehydrate them and administer various medications during the extraction process, which is moving very slowly," said a member of the French Civil Security.
The rescue team in that area includes members of the French Civil Security and American responders from the Fairfax County Urban Search and Rescue Team in Virginia, who the previous day rescued a mother and her 9-month-old baby.
Before extracting the family members, rescuers prepared intravenous drips and cleared debris.
Others remained beside the rubble searching for signs of life and communicating with their colleagues among the remains.
At least 33 people were rescued over the weekend, though tens of thousands remain missing, heightening fears that time is running out to find survivors.
According to specialists, after 72 hours following an earthquake, the odds of finding victims alive beneath the rubble drop dramatically. — REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police hunt Kembara driver who fled after Federal Highway crash [WATCH]
Article:
PETALING JAYA: Police are tracking down a 59-year-old driver who allegedly fled after causing a three-vehicle crash on the Federal Highway on June 20.
Petaling Jaya police said the incident, which recently went viral on social media, occurred at about 6.10pm on the stretch of the highway heading towards Kuala Lumpur from Shah Alam.
Initial investigations found that the man, who was driving a Perodua Kembara, allegedly drove recklessly before colliding with a car and a motorcycle.
The driver then abandoned his vehicle at the scene and fled.
View this post on Instagram
A post shared by New Straits Times (@nstonline)
The motorcyclist sustained minor injuries and was taken to Sunway Hospital for treatment.
Police said efforts are ongoing to trace the driver.
The case is being investigated under Section 42(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for reckless and dangerous driving.
Police urge anyone who witnessed the incident or has information to come forward by contacting the nearest police station or the investigating officer, Sergeant Kumaran Murugaya of the Petaling Jaya Traffic Enforcement and Investigation department, at 03-7966 2222.
They also reminded all road users to comply with traffic laws, warning that strict action would be taken against those who violate road regulations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sovereign investors with US$29tril pivot to energy assets, flag dollar fears
Article:
LONDON: Sovereign wealth funds and central banks managing US$29 trillion in assets are turning to energy assets and raising concerns about the dollar, in a portfolio reassessment driven by unprecedented geopolitical shifts, according to an Invesco survey published on Monday.
The survey of 90 sovereign wealth funds and 54 central banks showed an increasing focus on diversification and investment portfolios that can "take a hit and still hold it together" amid trade tariffs, closed shipping channels and wars in Ukraine and the Middle East.
Some 80 per cent of those polled said energy security and energy transition infrastructure were the most credible investments for making their portfolios more resilient and infrastructure reached nine per cent of sovereign wealth fund assets in 2026.
The race to build energy-hungry AI infrastructure added to the appeal, the report by the global investment management firm found.
"In a world of inflation shocks, geopolitical fragmentation and more concentrated markets, investors are rethinking old assumptions about diversification and redesigning portfolios to withstand a wider range of outcomes," Invesco head of research Benjamin Jones said.
"Resilience is becoming a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have."
The positive bond-equity correlation in recent years has also eroded reliance on bonds for diversification, with more investors focusing on liquidity and real assets.
DOLLAR, DEBT AND RISK
Concerns about the dollar were "widespread and deepening" and 61 per cent of central banks polled also said that US debt levels negatively impact the dollar's long-term position as a reserve asset, up from 20 per cent in 2024.
While the US-Israeli war with Iran has helped lift the dollar three per cent this year, analysts say US policy uncertainty and high debt mean the currency could weaken over the long term.
The lack of a credible dollar alternative is likely to make any shift away from it incremental, but 29 per cent of those in the Invesco survey said the dollar's reserve currency status will be weaker in five years, up from 12 per cent in 2022.
Several institutions also reported reviewing their reliance on US-based custodians, counterparties and clearing infrastructure due to geopolitical tensions, Invesco said.
One European central bank said it had already replaced its US custodian. A Latin American central bank said it was setting up new non-US custodial relationships to prepare for a "worst-case scenario".
But one central bank respondent said any such move was fraught: "This act in and of itself could be interpreted as hostile by the US."
One-third of those polled, meanwhile, said they intended to boost gold holdings as part of the diversification trend.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Looting, theft in Venezuela's earthquake zone add to tragedy
Article:
LA GUAIRA: Not even the cables inside the small store remained intact. The earth had barely stopped shaking when the looting and theft began in the area most devastated by Venezuela's double earthquake.
Outbreaks of looting have hit the coastal state of La Guaira – neighbouring Caracas – much of which is now a vast mountain of rubble after Wednesday's disaster.
In a video circulating on social media, a group of people pass around boxes of appliances from a collapsed store. Other videos appear to show looted boxes perched on car roofs or on top of motorcycles.
Online accusations are also circulating against police and military personnel who critics allege have been stealing from homes or even from the dead.
A branch of a major pharmacy chain was ransacked, as were supermarkets and other businesses, residents say.
Some attribute this situation to so-called disaster opportunism but others point to the hunger and destitution of those who have lost everything in a country already in chronic crisis before the quakes.
"Is it fair that our people are devouring each other?" lamented 71-year-old Maria Esther Bernal, who rented shops to Chinese merchants, all of which were looted.
"They even took the wiring."
She said a Chinese man had died in a store next door.
"They were stepping over his body to loot. It was a supermarket," she said.
Venezuelans have not hidden their anger at the slow and meagre aid coming from the authorities after the twin earthquakes that killed at least 1,450 and left tens of thousands more missing.
They are demanding not only rescue efforts in La Guaira but also improved security and assistance with food, water and medicine.
The government militarised the state and restricted access to those with a safe-conduct pass that must be obtained from the military in Caracas.
"There's nothing here," 72-year-old Zulay de Carvajal told AFP.
"They stole everything: our clothes, shoes, utensils, pots, cups, glasses."
"We found a disaster," said her son, Gregory Carvajal, 37.
"We were removing bodies, and at that moment, they were looting. People were going crazy, looting, taking everything."
[[nid:1475536]]
In another neighbourhood of La Guaira, similar looting broke out.
Some have been siphoning fuel from cars; others are impersonating firefighters to take advantage of the disaster.
There are reports of all kinds of crimes.
A video circulating on social media shows a man expelling a soldier and another official from his home after finding them scavenging around.
"They keep taking things, I can't stand it," protested the person recording with their phone. The officials told him they were only checking if people were inside.
"Get out, get out, they've looted everything."
La Guaira had already been devastated in 1999 by rains and massive landslides that swept away whole neighbourhoods and left more than 10,000 dead.
And at that time, there were also outbreaks of crime, said Marino Alvarado, a former coordinator of the human rights NGO Provea.
"It's not surprising that we can find three situations that also happened during the landslides," he said.
"Crime; two, police abuse, which is now beginning to be denounced; and three, police or military officials also participating in the looting."
After one of the Farmatodo pharmacy chain's branches was looted in La Guaira, the company cleaned the premises with the help of the community. A primary care clinic now operates there.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran urges US to set timeline for 'unconditional' Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon
Article:
ISTANBUL: Iran urged the US on Sunday to set a timetable for Israel's "unconditional" withdrawal from occupied Lebanese territories under a war-ending memorandum of understanding signed between Teheran and Washington, Anadolu Ajansi reported.
"Ending the war and military operations of the Zionist regime against Lebanon, as well as the withdrawal of occupiers from all occupied Lebanese territories, is a necessary condition for reaching a final and sustainable agreement to establish stability in the region," Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei said in a news briefing carried by ISNA News Agency.
Baqaei said Teheran considers safeguarding Lebanon's sovereignty and territorial integrity, as well as "the dignity and security of all Lebanese people", as "essential" for the durability of any agreement related to ending the war with the US.
Iran has placed "ending the war and military operations of the Zionist regime in Lebanon alongside ending the war against Iran" at the top of its demands in both the April ceasefire understanding and the June 18 memorandum, he added.
Baqaei also said Iran expects Washington to fulfil its commitments under the memorandum and "take all necessary measures to force the Zionist regime to stop any aggression and military operations against all Lebanese regions."
He added that Teheran is calling for "the swift determination of a timetable for unconditional withdrawal from occupied Lebanese territories."
Meanwhile, Iran's Parliament Speaker and chief negotiator Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf said in a phone call with Lebanese Parliament Speaker Nabih Berri that Teheran is "seriously pursuing" the end of the war in Lebanon.
According to Iranian media, Qalibaf said one of the main issues discussed during last week's talks with the US in Switzerland was "ending the war in Lebanon and restoring the country's sovereignty and territorial integrity."
He said the issue forms a central part of the first clause of the Islamabad memorandum and that, following Iran's objections over violations of the provision, it was agreed to establish a conflict-control unit between Iran, the US and Lebanon to monitor implementation.
"Our goal is ending the war in Lebanon, returning displaced people to their homes, ending the occupation and securing the withdrawal of the Zionist regime from Lebanese territory," Qalibaf said.
The developments come as Iran and the US continue efforts to implement the broader 14-point understanding reached after weeks of regional military escalation and diplomatic negotiations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil climbs following renewed US, Iran strikes in Middle East
Article:
SINGAPORE: Oil prices rose on Monday following days of tit-for-tat strikes by the US and Iran in the Middle East that underscored the fragility of their interim peace deal and again slowed energy shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
Brent crude futures climbed 52 cents, or 0.67 per cent, to US$72.51 a barrel by 2313 GMT while US West Texas Intermediate crude was at US$69.94 a barrel, up 71 cents, or 1.03 per cent.
Brent crude fell 10.6 per cent last week, its third weekly decline, after crude shipments through the strait rose last week to their highest level since the US-Israeli conflict with Iran began in February.
However, traffic has since slowed following renewed attacks on ships in the strait from Thursday, including a Qatar-linked oil tanker, that triggered strikes from the US and Iran in the worst escalation since they signed an interim peace deal.
"The market is likely to re-evaluate its assumption of a quick recovery of oil supply from the Persian Gulf," ANZ analysts said in a note.
Capping oil price gains, Iran and the US agreed to halt recent hostilities in the Gulf and renew talks in Qatar regarding their dispute over the Strait of Hormuz, Axios reported on Sunday. Reuters could not immediately confirm the report.
Saudi oil giant Aramco resumed crude oil loadings on Friday at its Ras Tanura terminal, west of the Strait of Hormuz, after they were halted for nearly four months, joining a rush to move cargoes after Middle East producers ramped up oil and gas output and exports ahead of the interim deal.
"Despite the US-Iran deal marking an inflection point for oil markets, physical flows are constrained by tanker backlogs, damaged infrastructure and production shut-ins," ANZ analysts said.
"It could take the remainder of the year before supply is near pre-conflict levels."
Loadings at Aramco's Ras Tanura terminal continued, even after a helicopter belonging to the company crashed on Sunday in Ras Tanura on the country's east coast on the Gulf, killing 14 nationals. The cause of the crash was unknown, the state news agency reported.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UK scraps plans to replace destroyers with focus on drone warships
Article:
LONDON: Britain will scrap plans to replace its ageing destroyers and will instead procure at least six "Common Combat Vessels" to serve as control hubs for uncrewed systems, the Ministry of Defence said on Sunday.
The Royal Navy's six Type 45 destroyers are due to be retired by the end of 2038, and original plans envisioned them being replaced by a next-generation Type 83 class.
However, under the long-awaited Defence Investment Plan, the warships will now be replaced by hybrid Common Combat Vessels (CCV), reflecting a broader shift towards drone warfare and frontline capabilities.
"These Common Combat Vessels will provide our dedicated sailors with hybrid ships that are designed and built for the increasing threats we face," Defence Secretary Dan Jarvis said in a statement.
"Developed with exceptional British innovators, the new ships will be British-built, supporting jobs across the nation and giving the Royal Navy a capability built for modern warfare."
The ministry said the CCVs would coordinate uncrewed systems in the air, on the surface and under the sea.
Delivery was expected from the early 2030s and when in service the vessels would work alongside crewed frigates as well as other planned autonomous vessels.
Earlier this month, former defence minister John Healey stepped down after disagreements over the Defence Investment Plan, which will lay out the funding for military equipment and services. He said the government had failed to commit the resources needed to keep Britain safe.
Since taking over, Jarvis has sought to refocus the plan on near-term priorities, including new high-speed boats for commandos and advanced strike drones.
Housing Secretary Steve Reed said on Sunday that the long-awaited strategy would be unveiled within days.
"We have to be ready for any future war and what that will be like, not whatever the last war was like," Reed told Sky News.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Factories Are Returning. Workers Are Not.
Article:
For much of the past three decades, conventional wisdom held that the United States had entered a post-industrial age.
Factories would gradually move abroad, manufacturing would occupy a smaller share of the economy, and America's future would be built upon services, finance, technology and innovation.
Recent developments suggest that this assumption may no longer be entirely accurate.
According to data from the United States Census Bureau, manufacturing construction spending has more than doubled compared with pre-pandemic levels, driven largely by investments in semiconductors, batteries and advanced manufacturing.
New industrial facilities are being built across the country, while billions of dollars are flowing into strategic sectors considered critical to national competitiveness and economic security.
At first glance, this appears to vindicate those who have long argued for a stronger industrial base.
President Donald Trump has made manufacturing revival a central pillar of his political and economic agenda. Yet it is important to recognise that this story is not entirely Trump's story.
While President Trump has made reindustrialisation a central political objective, many of the forces driving current manufacturing expansion, including the CHIPS and Science Act, supply chain restructuring following the pandemic, and strategic competition between the United States and China, predate his return to office.
The resurgence of manufacturing reflects a broader structural shift that spans multiple administrations and enjoys support across much of the American political spectrum.
Nevertheless, a closer examination reveals a paradox. American manufacturing is returning. Manufacturing jobs are not. This distinction may prove to be one of the defining economic realities of our era.
The Paradox Of Industrial Success
Historically, industrialisation and employment growth moved together. The great manufacturing centres of Detroit, Pittsburgh, Cleveland and Milwaukee became engines of prosperity because factories created large numbers of middle class jobs.
Industrial expansion translated directly into employment opportunities, rising wages and social mobility.
Today's manufacturing economy operates very differently. The industries driving America's industrial revival are not textile mills, automobile assembly lines or consumer goods factories.
They are semiconductor fabrication plants, advanced aerospace facilities, precision machinery producers and highly specialised electronics manufacturers.
These sectors generate enormous economic value. They produce sophisticated products that command high margins and strengthen national competitiveness.
However, they also require far fewer workers than traditional manufacturing industries.
A modern semiconductor fabrication facility may cost tens of billions of dollars to construct, yet employ only a few thousand highly skilled engineers, technicians and specialists.
Advanced production increasingly relies on automation, robotics, artificial intelligence and precision machinery. The result is a manufacturing sector capable of producing more output while employing fewer people.
This is not necessarily a sign of economic weakness. On the contrary, it reflects the increasing sophistication of modern industry.
The challenge lies elsewhere. Political expectations often remain rooted in the industrial economy of the twentieth century, while economic reality increasingly reflects the technological economy of the twenty first.
Beyond Tariffs: The Real Drivers Of Reindustrialisation
Public debates frequently focus on tariffs as the principal instrument of industrial revival.
Tariffs undoubtedly matter. They can encourage domestic production, reduce import dependence and provide temporary protection for strategic sectors. However, the deeper forces shaping American manufacturing extend far beyond trade policy.
The most significant driver is geopolitics. The strategic competition between the United States and China has fundamentally altered the way policymakers view industrial capacity.
Semiconductors are no longer merely commercial products. They are strategic assets. Supply chains are no longer simply economic networks.
They are increasingly viewed through the lens of national security.
This explains why governments are willing to support industries that may not always maximise short term economic efficiency.
Resilience, security and technological sovereignty have become policy objectives in their own right.
The revival of American manufacturing is therefore not merely an economic story. It is also a story about statecraft.
Washington's objective is not simply to produce more goods. It is to ensure that critical technologies, strategic industries and key supply chains remain under trusted jurisdictions.
In this context, industrial policy represents a response to a changing geopolitical landscape as much as it does an economic strategy.
Why This Matters For Malaysia
The American experience offers an important lesson for countries seeking to strengthen their own industrial sectors.
Industrial policy should not be measured solely by the number of factories built or the value of investment announcements. Equally important are questions of productivity, innovation, technological capability and workforce preparedness.
Malaysia faces a similar challenge. The National Semiconductor Strategy and the expansion of advanced manufacturing promise substantial investment and technological upgrading.
Yet policymakers must recognise that 21st century industrialisation is increasingly capital intensive.
The key measure of success is not merely investment value, but whether productivity, wages, technological capabilities and local supply chains advance alongside it.
The objective should not simply be more industry. The objective should be better industry.
The future belongs not to those who produce the most goods, but to those who create the greatest value.
The Real Test Of Industrial Strategy
The question confronting the United States is therefore not whether manufacturing can return. It already is.
The more important question is whether the benefits of this new industrial economy can be distributed broadly enough to sustain social cohesion and political legitimacy.
The factories of the future may be filled with robots, algorithms and highly skilled engineers.
But the success of any industrial strategy will ultimately be judged not by machines alone, but by whether citizens believe they have a meaningful stake in the prosperity those machines create.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Carbon credits to fund Pahang peatland restoration
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Pahang Peatland Restoration Project (PPRP) is banking on future carbon credit sales to finance its 60-year restoration programme covering 96,569 hectares of peat swamp forest, as it progresses towards international certification.
Carbon credit project expert and oversight committee member Dr Jane Koh said PPRP is pursuing certification under the Verra Verified Carbon Standard (VCS) and Climate, Community and Biodiversity (CCB) Standards.
Proceeds from future carbon credit sales will be channelled back into peatland restoration efforts, fire prevention initiatives and community development programmes, creating a self-sustaining funding model for long-term conservation.
The PPRP is designed as a sustainable economic model to conserve and restore the largest contiguous peatland in Peninsular Malaysia.
To achieve this over its project lifespan, the PPRP needs a robust and sustainable economic engine, which is exactly where carbon finance comes in.
Koh said the project is structured around a 60-year conservation model, with carbon finance providing a sustainable funding mechanism for long-term restoration work including peat rewetting, fire prevention and community development initiatives.
"Carbon credits are the mechanism that funds the real, on-the-ground work in terms of rewetting the peat, preventing fires and supporting the local communities," she told Business Times.
Koh said the project had already secured upfront financing to begin restoration work ahead of the issuance of any emission reduction credits.
Once operational, the project is expected to prevent and remove about 3.1 million tonnes of carbon emissions annually, with proceeds from carbon credit sales reinvested into conservation efforts.
She said peat swamp forests are among the world's most effective terrestrial carbon sinks, despite covering only about three per cent of global land area.
Beyond carbon storage, peatlands also regulate water flows by absorbing and gradually releasing excess water, helping reduce flood risks during monsoon seasons and mitigating drought during dry periods.
Koh said decades of drainage and logging had left the Pahang peatland landscape vulnerable to fires and degradation, making early intervention critical to prevent permanent ecological damage.
She described the project as the first nature-based carbon initiative in Peninsular Malaysia seeking certification under internationally recognised standards.
Addressing concerns surrounding the voluntary carbon market, Koh said the project welcomed external scrutiny and had adopted multiple layers of monitoring and independent verification.
She said satellite imagery, drones, real-time field sensors and dedicated fire response teams were used alongside the nationally harmonised Spatial Monitoring and Reporting Tool to monitor fire risks, illegal activities and track biodiversity.
The project is currently undergoing validation under Verra's VCS and CCB standards, with independent auditors reviewing its technical calculations, field data and stakeholder engagement processes before any carbon credits can be issued.
On community engagement, Koh said the project had adopted a rights-based approach that prioritised Free, Prior and Informed Consent with Orang Asli communities living around the project area.
She said more than half of the current project workforce comprises members of the Orang Asli Jakun community, while a grievance redress mechanism has also been established.
The project is also developing a long-term community benefit programme known as the Ranok Resilience Initiatives in collaboration with local communities.
Koh said the project is progressing through Verra's validation and verification process and has already received interest from potential corporate buyers seeking high-integrity carbon credits to support their net-zero strategies.
Nature-based carbon projects, she said, remain challenging due to the need to balance ecological restoration, scientific monitoring, stakeholder engagement and regulatory compliance over several decades.
Public awareness of nature-based carbon business models remains relatively low in Malaysia, making education and stakeholder engagement an important part of the project's long-term development, she added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Yayasan Peneraju targets IC design talent push
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Yayasan Peneraju is ramping up efforts to develop Bumiputera talent in integrated circuit (IC) design as part of its broader strategy to support Malaysia's ambition of becoming a global semiconductor hub.
Chief executive officer Ibrahim Sani said the agency aims to produce 2,000 Bumiputera IC designers, representing 20 per cent of the government's target to develop 10,000 semiconductor talents nationwide.
To achieve this, Yayasan Peneraju will launch a pilot programme involving 150 Bumiputera participants, with training delivered across five to six institutions in collaboration with industry players.
"The pilot programme will help us assess the effectiveness of the curriculum before expanding it on a larger scale," he told Business Times.
Participants will undergo six months of technical, practical and on-the-job training using industry-standard platforms such as ARM Architecture, Synopsys and Cadence.
The programme will cater to both fresh graduates and working professionals seeking to reskill for careers in chip design.
Ibrahim said Malaysia has long established itself in semiconductor back-end manufacturing but must now strengthen capabilities in front-end IC design to remain globally competitive.
He noted that demand for skills in artificial intelligence (AI), cybersecurity, cloud computing and data analytics is also growing rapidly, making advanced technology capabilities increasingly essential across industries.
"Historically, Bumiputera participation in chip design has been relatively low, not because of capability, but due to limited exposure to opportunities in the industry," he said.
Yayasan Peneraju plans to work closely with agencies such as Bumiputera Agenda Steering Unit (Teraju) and Majlis Amanah Rakyat (Mara), as well as semiconductor companies, to identify and train suitable candidates.
The semiconductor initiative forms part of Yayasan Peneraju's broader transformation under its Roadmap to 2030, which shifts the organisation's focus from wealth creation to value creation.
Since its establishment in 2012, the agency has empowered more than 100,000 Bumiputera talents through education, professional certification and skills development programmes.
During Ibrahim's three-year tenure, the size of Yayasan Peneraju's talent ecosystem nearly doubled from about 50,000 to almost 100,000 individuals.
He said the growth was driven by improved talent sourcing and selection, greater use of digital platforms and the adoption of AI to accelerate application assessments and enhance operational efficiency, all without increasing the organisation's workforce.
Beyond technology, Ibrahim said Yayasan Peneraju will continue strengthening the country's professional talent pipeline, particularly in accounting.
As of June this year, the agency has supported 6,713 talents in the technology sector and about 3,370 in professional qualifications.
He said the transformation in Bumiputera participation in the accounting profession as one of Yayasan Peneraju's most significant achievements.
In 2012, there was roughly one Bumiputera professional accountant for every 500 non-Bumiputera accountants.
By 2025, newly-qualified Bumiputera accountants had reached parity with their non-Bumiputera counterparts, while in 2026 Bumiputera professionals joining the Malaysian Institute of Accountants surpassed non-Bumiputera entrants for the first time.
"We have shown that sustained and focused talent development can create meaningful change. We believe we can replicate this success in emerging sectors such as semiconductor design," Ibrahim said.
Yayasan Peneraju has established a dedicated Leadership Division to identify the top 10 per cent of talents within its Talent Bank for advanced leadership programmes in collaboration with institutions including INSEAD and Melbourne Business School.
The initiative supports the agency's goal of producing 10,000 Bumiputera value creators by 2030.
Leaders are expected to drive innovation, create businesses, generate employment and contribute to national development.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysian home renovation demand rising post-pandemic on lifestyle shift, says Topmix CEO
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's home improvement and renovation market, valued at RM47.7 billion in 2024 and projected to grow to RM59.2 billion by 2029, is poised for sustained expansion as more homeowners invest in upgrading their living spaces to match changing lifestyles and personal preferences.
Topmix Bhd managing director and Luma International Sdn Bhd chief executive officer Jack Teo Quek Siang said the trend has accelerated since the Covid-19 pandemic, backed supported by rising household incomes, rapid urbanisation and stronger demand for personalised homes.
He said more Malaysians are opting to renovate existing homes rather than merely maintaining them, with younger homeowners in particular placing greater emphasis on aesthetics, functionality and lifestyle-driven design.
"Since the pandemic, customer behaviour has changed.
"More homeowners are renovating their houses, and demand has grown rapidly compared with before the pandemic.
"Income levels have also increased, and consumers, especially the younger generation, are placing greater importance on lifestyle and personal preferences," he said on Tuesday, after inking agreements with RHB Bank, RHB Islamic Bank and SP Setia Bhd for its myRuma digital renovation platform.
Teo said continued urbanisation will is expected to sustain demand for renovation services and interior building materials as more Malaysians upgrade their homes.
He said added that the secondary housing market is another key demand driver, as resale homes typically require refurbishment or remodelling to meet new owners' preferences.
Beyond residential properties, Teo said renovation demand is also growing in the commercial segment, particularly among food and beverage outlets, as well as and hotels seeking to enhance customer experience through modern and aesthetically appealing interiors.
"The demand is growing across the renovation industry as businesses also want more attractive and aesthetic spaces," he said.
To keep pace with changing consumer preferences, Teo said Topmix regularly refreshes its product portfolio by introducing new designs and expanding its product offerings.
He said the company works closely with its upstream partners in Europe to stay ahead of global design trends, allowing it to introduce new collections ahead of market demand.
"Our customers and interior designers are always looking for fresh new ideas and new designs. Product innovation allows us to adapt to changing market trends and customer expectations.
These include new product ranges, component systems and, most recently, the launch of the myRuma digital renovation platform, which expands Topmix reach into the homeowner segment." added Teo.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rising insurance premiums squeeze e-hailing drivers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: E-hailing drivers are increasingly concerned over soaring insurance and takaful costs after daily premiums soar since the past few years, raising fears of further pressure on already thin earnings.
Daily e-hailing insurance premiums are currently vary by insurers and type of coverage, but the market has seen a sharp increase over the last four years.
Some observers said the industry's benchmark daily premium has risen significantly to around RM5.50 per day in 2025 from about RM3.26 per day in 2022.
Several insurers are now charging between RM7 and RM7.50 per day, they added.
Industry players, insurers and driver representatives acknowledge the growing financial burden but remain divided on whether the increases are justified and what measures are needed to ensure the long-term sustainability of the e-hailing ecosystem.
The Malaysian Takaful Association (MTA) said it is aware of concerns raised by e-hailing drivers over the affordability of insurance and takaful protection.
MTA chief executive officer Mohd Radzuan Mohamed said the issue had been raised through industry engagements facilitated by Bank Negara Malaysia, particularly concerns over rising protection costs and the impact on drivers' earnings and participation in the sector.
"These concerns have been recognised as part of the broader discussions within the e-hailing insurance/takaful ecosystem industry working group," he told Business Times.
Too Early To Predict
Mohd Radzuan said it would be premature to speculate on whether insurance and takaful costs for e-hailing drivers would continue rising, stabilise or moderate over the next 12 to 24 months.
He said pricing trends would depend on several factors, including claims experience, risk patterns, market conditions and the outcome of ongoing industry initiatives.
However, Mohd Radzuan said the industry is actively exploring measures to improve affordability over time.
"These include the development of more flexible and usage-based coverage models, improved driver status verification mechanisms to support more accurate risk assessment, and interim measures such as optional excess structures that may provide drivers with lower contribution options.
"The shared objective among stakeholders is to develop a framework that is affordable, accessible and sustainable, while ensuring that drivers continue to receive appropriate protection," he said.
Higher Risks Driving Concerns
The General Insurance Association of Malaysia (PIAM) said commercial e-hailing operations generally carry a higher risk profile due to extended road exposure and higher mileage, resulting in more frequent and severe claims.
The association said rising costs associated with vehicle repairs, property damage and third-party bodily injury compensation have also contributed to pressure on insurers.
"This elevated risk profile, coupled with the rising costs of property damage, vehicle repairs, and compensation for third party death and bodily injuries, has necessitated periodic premium adjustments by insurers to ensure the long-term sustainability of this coverage," it said.
PIAM said it is working closely with Bank Negara, government agencies and industry stakeholders to ensure premium structures remain transparent, sustainable and supported by data.
It added that the industry is exploring long-term measures such as telematics adoption, enhanced industry data-sharing and initiatives to encourage safer driving behaviour.
Drivers Seek Greater Transparency
Gabungan eHailing Malaysia said driver concerns extend beyond the increase in premiums itself and include questions over whether such increases are proportionate and supported by sufficient industry data.
Its chief activist Jose Rizal said insurers have consistently cited higher accident frequencies, claims costs and loss ratios as reasons for premium adjustments, but driver representatives have yet to see sufficient industry-wide data to independently assess those claims.
"GEM believes the immediate priority is not necessarily price intervention, but greater transparency and regulatory scrutiny of the factors driving these increases.
"Similar explanations have also been conveyed during our engagements with Bank Negara," he said.
Jose said transparency would help ensure greater trust among drivers who are required to purchase insurance coverage in order to operate on e-hailing platforms.
"As representatives of platform workers, we have yet to see sufficient industry-wide data that would allow stakeholders to independently assess the validity and scale of these claims.
"If insurers, through PIAM, can provide comprehensive and aggregated data on accident rates, claims frequencies, claims severity, underwriting performance, and loss ratios specific to the e-hailing sector, then discussions can be conducted based on evidence rather than assumptions," he said.
Impact On Driver Participation
Ride-hailing platform inDrive said rising insurance costs are becoming a growing concern for drivers, particularly those who depend on e-hailing as a key source of income.
The company said higher recurring costs could affect drivers' take-home earnings and influence how often they choose to drive, especially among part-time drivers.
It called for continued collaboration between regulators, insurers, platform operators and driver groups to ensure protection remains affordable while maintaining safety and compliance standards across the sector.
"This could include exploring more flexible, usage-based or affordable insurance options, greater transparency on how premiums are determined, and continued dialogue with driver communities to ensure policy decisions reflect the realities on the ground.
"The goal should not be to reduce safety or compliance standards, but to ensure that the cost of meeting those standards remains fair and sustainable for the people who keep the sector moving," inDrive added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: UK's likely next leader Burnham to unveil plan to shift power from London
Article:
LONDON: Andy Burnham, the Labour lawmaker expected to replace Keir Starmer as British prime minister, will outline his vision for Britain on Monday, his office said, promising to change how the nation is governed with power moving from London to the regions.
Burnham, who returned to Westminster earlier this month after winning a parliamentary seat, is currently the only declared candidate to take over from Starmer and could be installed in Downing Street within weeks.
Starmer announced last week he would step down, just two years after winning a huge parliamentary majority for Labour, as his popularity sank.
Burnham, who rose to prominence as mayor of Greater Manchester and has been dubbed the "King of the North", will use Monday's speech to make devolving power to regions and local communities his flagship proposal.
He will also commit to a 10-year mission to raise living standards through reindustrialisation, housing, infrastructure and reform of utilities. The focus would be not just on who governs Britain, but on changing how it is governed, his office said.
He will set out how he plans to "lift Britain back up to where it should be" and provide the "circuit-breaker it needs", alongside a pledge to reform public procurement to better support British jobs and industry.
If he takes office, Burnham will become Britain's seventh prime minister in a decade, with many in his party believing only he has the charisma and the vision to connect with voters and counter the rise of Nigel Farage's anti-immigration Reform UK party.
[[nid:1475556]]
FISCAL PRESSURES LIMIT SCOPE
However, with Britain's economy struggling from the impact of the war in Ukraine and more recently the energy shock of the US conflict with Iran, the scope to make radical spending changes is limited.
Burnham has previously said the government should "get beyond this thing of being in hock to the bond markets" but later said his comments had been misrepresented. He has also stepped back from earlier calls for large-scale nationalisation or a near-term return to the European Union.
Housing Minister Steve Reed said on Sunday Burnham would stick to Labour's commitments made before the 2024 election and also to the government's fiscal rules, including balancing day-to-day spending with tax revenues and reducing debt as a share of output.
"When it comes to the fundamentals, Andy has been clear he will stick to the fiscal rules that have delivered this country stability in the economy for the first time in over 15 years," he told Sky News.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump bashes Washington's likely next mayor, touts golf course project
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump lashed out Sunday at Washington's presumptive next mayor, calling her a communist who would destroy the capital city, even as he touted grandiose building projects including the refurbishment of a golf course.
Trump said in a Truth Social post that he would meet with Janeese Lewis George, who prevailed in the Democratic Party mayoral primary Tuesday, all but assuring that she will win November's election in the heavily Democratic city.
But Trump warned that he would not let Washington, which he claimed to have made a "safe and prestigious community" again with the deployment of National Guard troops, "be destroyed by a communist adherent".
Trump recently has ramped up his use of the word "communist" to describe political foes, including referring to three winners of Democratic primaries last week in New York City backed by leftist Mayor Zohran Mamdani as "godless communists".
Like Mamdani, Lewis George identifies as a democratic socialist, not a communist.
In a lengthy second post, Trump bragged about steps he has taken to beautify Washington, including resurfacing the bottom of the Reflecting Pool with an "American flag blue" coating used in swimming pools.
But since its installation, the coating has begun to peel off and algae has turned the water green – a persistent problem over the years.
[[nid:1475486]]
Repeating past claims, Trump accused "Criminal, Radical Left Vandals" of cutting the bottom and ripping up sections of coating.
He has not provided evidence for his allegations, but the National Park Service told AFP last week that five individuals had been arrested for vandalism.
Trump said the pool, in front of the Lincoln Memorial, would be drained and repaired after the July 4 holiday marking 250 years of American independence.
The president went on to say he planned to renovate a "dilapidated, worn out, and very dangerous" golf course in Washington called East Potomac Golf Links, located on an island in the Potomac River.
After touring the course Sunday, Trump said "it was determined that, on this fantastic site, with water and unparalleled views of D.C.'s monuments, we will build one of the greatest golf courses anywhere in the world".
When renovations are complete, East Potomac could host major tournaments including the US Open, the former real estate developer said.
The golf course plan is the latest example of Trump's attempts to impose his personal imprint on the capital's architecture.
Since his second term began, Trump has torn down the White House's East Wing with plans to build an enormous ballroom and proposed a huge arch on the far side of the Potomac, near Arlington National Cemetery for fallen American soldiers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hizbollah-allied Lebanese parliament head says US-Israel-Lebanon deal 'will not pass'
Article:
BEIRUT: Lebanese Parliament Speaker and Hizbollah ally Nabih Berri said early Monday that the trilateral framework agreement between Lebanon, Israel and the United States would not pass as it did not guarantee Lebanon's rights.
"This agreement will not pass, and it will not be implemented in its current form," Berri said in a statement shared by his party, the Amal movement, adding that it was "an agreement of 'dictates', not an agreement that preserves Lebanon's rights."
The deal paves the way for Lebanese-Israeli peace and conditions Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon on Hizbollah's disarmament.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Experts optimistic LRT3 can lure Malaysians out of their cars [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  As the Light Rail Transit 3  (LRT3) Shah Alam  Line prepares to welcome its first passengers today, public transport advocates are hopeful the  rail corridor, backed by feeder buses and on-demand vans, will  draw more Malaysians away from their cars.
They believe the supporting network provides a solid starting point.
Malaysian Public Transport Users Association (4PAM) president Ajit Johl said the first-and- last-mile connectivity  for the Shah Alam Line — including 40 feeder buses, Rapid On-Demand vans and Park & Ride bays — appeared adequate for now and that operators would likely scale up capacity if needed.
On the ridership targets of 67,000  passengers daily in the first year and  118,000 within five years, he said the projected growth was reasonable and achievable.
"A 43 per cent increase over five years works out to roughly eight per cent annual growth, which would have been calculated based on past experiences with similar rail lines."
He attributed the expected growth to several  factors, including  population growth, as well as transit-oriented developments (TODs) planned along the rail corridor and future rationalisation of  subsidies that could push car users to switch to public transport.
On whether the fare structure and train frequency  would be attractive enough to convert  car users into long-term LRT commuters, Ajit said the pricing was reasonable.
At peak hours, the service will run every eight minutes, while during off-peak hours, it will run every 10 to 15 minutes.
LRT fares range from 80sen to RM4.90.  The feeder bus fare costs RM1 while the on-demand van fare costs RM2.
[[nid:1475580]]
"Public transport is heavily subsidised by the government. There are many incentives  provided by the government,  such as a small fare for last-mile services.
"We cannot expect free stuff, as there are operational cost issues," he said.
Meanwhile, transport analyst Dr Rosli Azad Khan said it would not be easy to  wean Malaysians off  cars.
He said Malaysians did not bear the full social cost of driving as  road maintenance and carbon emissions were largely "paid" for by the government.
He said first-mile and last-mile connectivity was the single biggest concern of public transport.
On fares, Rosli said public transport needed to remain substantially cheaper than owning  a car, adding that Malaysians often underestimated the true cost of car ownership because such costs were spread across loan instalments, insurance and maintenance rather than paid upfront.
He suggested that Malaysia follow other countries by bundling public transport into seamless ticketing systems with daily or monthly fare caps to make rail more financially attractive.
He pointed to TODs as another critical factor, adding that successful rail systems in cities such as Hong Kong, Singapore and Tokyo worked because housing, offices, retail and services were concentrated around stations.
The Shah Alam Line was launched  by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim yesterday.
The project, involving an investment of RM16.63 billion, is among the government's strategic investments in strengthening public transport infrastructure.
The Shah Alam Line spans 37.8km with 20 stations connecting Bandar Utama in Petaling Jaya to Johan Setia in Klang.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel detonates tunnel, strikes south Lebanon as Hizbollah says has right to self-defence
Article:
BEIRUT: The Israeli army destroyed an extensive tunnel in southern Lebanon on Sunday, with Lebanese state media reporting strikes in the area and Iran-backed Hizbollah saying it reserves the right to respond to the attacks.
The incidents came despite a trilateral framework agreement signed by Lebanon and Israel under US sponsorship on Friday to pave the way for peace between the two countries and disarm Iran-backed Hizbollah.
Lebanon's state-run National News Agency (NNA) reported several strikes Sunday, including on the southern city of Nabatieh, with the Lebanese health ministry saying two people were wounded by an Israeli stun grenade in the south.
"The tunnel, stretching more than 200 metres and reaching a depth of over 25 metres, contained hundreds of weapons as well as several launch shafts intended to target the State of Israel and its civilians," Katz and Netanyahu said in a joint statement.
"Israel informed the United States and the US representative in Lebanon in advance of the destruction of the infrastructure."
An AFP reporter in the coastal city of Tyre, situated about 10 kilometres (six miles) from the location of the detonation near the village of Majdal Zoun, saw smoke rising in the distance.
He said residents in towns south of Tyre left after hearing reports in the Lebanese media that Israel might detonate nearby.
In response to the attacks on Sunday, Hizbollah said it "reiterates that what the enemy has done is a blatant violation of the ceasefire to which it has adhered until now, and that it is monitoring and tracking these violations, and reserves its right to defend its homeland and its people."
PRESSURE ON HIZBOLLAH
The Israeli army said one of its soldiers "fell in combat" in southern Lebanon, and later said it killed a "Hizbollah fighter" who had clashed with its forces.
Hizbollah drew the country into the Middle East war in March with rocket fire aimed at Israel to avenge the killing of Iran's supreme leader in US-Israeli strikes.
Israel responded with massive airstrikes and a ground invasion.
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun told his US counterpart Donald Trump on Saturday that his country "would assume its responsibilities" in implementing the agreement.
The deal makes any Israeli withdrawal from occupied Lebanese land conditional on Beirut disarming Hizbollah by creating "pilot zones" that the Lebanese military will take over.
Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu called the deal historic for his country and a blow to Teheran.
Israel's Defence Minister Israel Katz has insisted troops will stay in Lebanon so long as Hizbollah remains armed.
INTERNAL CONFLICT
Hizbollah strongly opposed the talks with Israel from the start and rejects the agreement.
Leader Naim Qassem said on Saturday that the group would treat the deal as "null and void" and described it as "a surrender of sovereignty."
On Sunday, an AFP correspondent saw signs reading "Lebanon first" being burned along Beirut's airport road, which borders the city's southern suburbs, a Hizbollah stronghold, after previous billboards saying "thank you Iran" were removed.
Hizbollah lawmaker Hassan Fadlallah said Sunday that what "the authorities have done amounts to sedition aimed at pushing the country into chaos and shifting the conflict from one with the enemy to an internal conflict."
Lebanese Parliament Speaker and Hizbollah ally Nabih Berri said the deal would "not pass" in its current form, calling it a dictate that does not preserve "Lebanon's rights", vowing to confront it politically and warning against internal strife.
"It is ten times worse than the May 17, 1983 agreement," he added, referring to the last time Lebanon and Israel signed a peace deal, which was then cancelled after pressure from Syria and its Lebanese allies.
Hizbollah repeatedly asked Lebanese authorities to link themselves to Iran's negotiations to end its war with the US, while Teheran has insisted any ceasefire for the Middle East war should include Lebanon.
In a phone conversation with Berri, the Iranian parliament speaker and head of Teheran's negotiating delegation Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf said "our goal is to end the war in Lebanon, return the refugees to their homes and remove the occupation and the withdrawal of the Zionist regime from the Lebanese territory."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Council tests Semak Berita fact-checking portal
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Media Council will trial fact-checking portal Semak Berita during the Johor election period.
The council said the initiative will be evaluated after the trial.
Disputed news that has been clarified will be published online at sb.majlismedia.my.
Participating media organisations will appoint primary and alternate verification officers.
When the credibility of content is disputed, the council will coordinate verification with relevant parties.
Procedural election matters, such as polling centres, voting processes and official results, will be referred to the Election Commission.
Council chairman Tan Sri Nallini Pathmanathan said the initiative addressed growing concerns about dubious and fabricated news, and the misuse of media identities during elections.
The council said false news could take the form of fabricated news graphics, screenshots, statements and election results that look convincing, and could be accompanied by the logos of legitimate news organisations.
Such content can be generated and manipulated using artificial intelligence.
The council said using the names and logos of news organisations to spread falsehoods undermines public trust in the media.
"The Malaysian Media Council is not an arbiter of political truth. We do not adjudicate manifestos, campaign claims or debates between political parties," Nallini said.
She said Semak Berita was a coordination platform for rapid verification and information sharing among stakeholders.
"The line we draw is straightforward: was this content genuinely published by the newsroom in question? That is a factual matter that can be verified quickly."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 10,000 tap early access to Budi Diesel
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
LANGKAWI: The early-access phase of the enhanced Budi Madani Diesel (Budi Diesel) subsidy scheme got off to a smooth start yesterday, benefiting around 10,000 people ahead of its full rollout on July 1.
Finance Minister II Datuk Seri Amir Hamzah Azizan said the four-day early access was introduced to ensure a seamless implementation of the scheme, which now allows vehicle owners to transfer their subsidised diesel quota to family members who regularly use their vehicles.
"The rollout includes small traders as well as company-owned private pickup trucks and jeeps," he told reporters after launching the ATV Safari tourism product here on Saturday.
Amir Hamzah said the improvement followed public feedback, particularly from rural Sabah and Sarawak where family members often purchase diesel on behalf of vehicle owners.
"We took note of the situation, where children sometimes buy diesel for their parents even though they are not the registered vehicle owners.
"Under the new system, vehicle owners can transfer their subsidised diesel quota to eligible family members through the Budi Madani Diesel portal," he added.
The scheme allows consumers to purchase subsidised diesel at RM2.15 per litre for a flexible quota of up to 200 litres, before the rate is adjusted to RM2.10 per litre starting Wednesday.
While welcoming the targeted subsidy, diesel vehicle owners said the monthly fuel quota remained insufficient for those who rely heavily on their vehicles.
Trader Mohammad Fazreen Idham Mohd Jais, 33, said the lower price would ease his monthly fuel bill, which previously ranged between RM1,000 and RM1,500.
"Before this, we really felt the impact of the diesel price hikes. But this new price helps diesel vehicle users a lot. I hope the government can maintain this price," he said.
Retired serviceman Rashid Razali, 45, also welcomed the subsidy, but urged the government to review the quota.
"I have no issue with the new price, but it would help if the quota could be increased.
"For people who genuinely depend on diesel, especially for interstate travel, the current allocation runs out quickly," he said.
Under the Budi Diesel programme, around 700,000 eligible private diesel vehicle owners will receive subsidised fuel through MyKad verification.
Recipients are entitled to a basic monthly quota of up to 200 litres, while eligible pickup truck and four-wheel-drive vehicle owners may apply for an additional 100 litres.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Chery boosts presence in Malaysia
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Chinese automaker Chery says it has expanded its presence in Malaysia to 60 dealerships nationwide and launched its 10th vehicle model, three years after re-entering the market.
The Tiggo 9 marks the company's 10th vehicle introduced in Malaysia and broadens its SUV range.
A key milestone in Chery's expansion is the development of the RM2.2 billion Chery Smart Auto Industrial Park in Lembah Beringin, Selangor.
The 80.94ha facility, which recently completed the topping-out of its first phase, is set to become the company's largest assembly plant in Malaysia.
"The automotive industry thrives on competition, and we welcome fair competition," Chery said.
"Ultimately, the strongest endorsement of any brand comes from its customers whose confidence in its products will continue to be the backbone of its growth in Malaysia."
Beyond its commercial expansion, Chery said it has continued to invest in customer engagement through its With Chery With Love community platform, which organises lifestyle, family and community activities for owners.
The company has also stepped up its environmental and community initiatives.
Earlier this year, it launched "Cherish the Nature", an 18-month mangrove restoration programme along the North-Central Selangor Coast in collaboration with the International Union for Conservation of Nature and the Malaysian Nature Society.
Chery also provided 50 vehicles to residents affected by the Putra Heights gas pipe explosion in Selangor last year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US military to help Venezuela airport, seaport reopen for aid
Article:
WASHINGTON: US military personnel were arriving in quake-hit Venezuela to help expand airport capacity and reopen a key seaport to support relief efforts, the US Southern Command said in a statement on Sunday.
Simon Bolivar International Airport, which serves the Venezuelan capital of Caracas, was partially reopened Saturday after suffering damage in Wednesday's twin quakes along the country's northern coast.
